summary: Gruber argues that a rumored systemwide UI redesign for iOS 19 would be the most impactful change Apple could make, far overshadowing any Siri or AI improvements. He draws parallels to past polarizing redesigns like Aqua and iOS 7, noting that radical visual changes always provoke strong negative reactions initially but often define industry trends for a decade. He contends that Apple's strength lies in leaders with great taste who trust their instincts rather than seeking consensus. The piece warns that playing it safe leads to stagnation, and that initial public reaction is a poor judge of design quality.
key_points: A new systemwide UI theme would dominate user attention far more than any AI or Siri improvements, because humans are fundamentally visual creatures
Past radical redesigns (Aqua, iOS 7) were initially polarizing but set industry-wide design trends that lasted a decade
Apple's identity depends on leaders with great taste who trust their instincts rather than avoiding controversy
Companies that only push out non-controversial changes end up in paralysis and stagnation
Initial public reaction to design changes is unreliable — most people lack the taste to judge something new and unfamiliar
money_quotes: Humans are fundamentally visual creatures and we notice visual changes. And, most humans are resistant to change.
Part of what makes Apple Apple is that the company is (or at least should be) led by people who both have great taste and trust their own instincts.
When a company only pushes out changes that avoid controversy, it leads to paralysis. Then stagnation.
Either Apple is never going to ship an altogether new UI theme, or they'll ship one and a large number of people will declare it utter garbage and proof that Apple has completely lost its way.
You either have taste or you don't, and most people don't, at least when judging something new and unfamiliar.
No one's taste is perfect. Even Steve Jobs pushed through a few clunkers.
A lot of people simply dislike change, even changes for the better.
key_insight: Radical UI redesigns are always initially polarizing, but Apple's greatness depends on trusting its taste and shipping bold visual changes rather than playing it safe into stagnation.
